# Pitch Deck Outline

This chapter provides the narrative structure for a presentation to funders. Each section maps to a slide or slide group. Copy this structure into your presentation tool of choice; the prose here is speaker-note level detail.

## Slide 1 — Hook

> "Twitter/X is owned by one person. Facebook is optimised for engagement, not truth. TikTok is a national security debate. Meanwhile, 10 million people are quietly building something different."

One image: a map of Fediverse nodes across Europe.

---

## Section 1 — The Problem (3–4 slides)

### 1a. The platform crisis
- Three platforms control the global public conversation.
- Their incentive structures are structurally misaligned with public interest: engagement over accuracy, growth over wellbeing, extraction over sustainability.
- The DSA creates obligations but not alternatives. Regulation without infrastructure is incomplete.

### 1b. The infrastructure gap
- The Fediverse runs on volunteer labour and small donations.
- Server costs, security maintenance, moderation tooling, and protocol development are chronically underfunded.
- One burned-out admin shutting down a large instance can displace tens of thousands of users overnight.
- There is no equivalent of the "internet infrastructure" investment that maintains DNS, TLS certificate authorities, or BGP routing — functions so critical that they have attracted both public and private investment.

### 1c. The governance gap
- No entity currently speaks for the Fediverse as a whole.
- Protocol development happens in the open but without coordinated resourcing.
- Public institutions (municipalities, broadcasters, universities) that want to deploy Fediverse infrastructure have no single point of entry, no SLA, no compliance guidance.

---

## Section 2 — The Solution (2–3 slides)

### 2a. What the Fediverse already is
- 10M+ active accounts across 20,000+ servers.
- ActivityPub is a W3C standard — the same standards body behind HTML and CSS.
- Growing institutional adoption: Deutsche Welle, NPR, the EU itself experimented with Mastodon.
- FOSS ecosystem: Mastodon, Pixelfed, PeerTube, Peertube, Lemmy, Misskey — each solving a different use case.

### 2b. What we are building
The **[Coalition Name]** is the coordinating body the Fediverse needs:

- A shared voice for the Fediverse in policy and funding arenas.
- A maintenance fund that pools resources for critical infrastructure.
- A governance framework that institutional partners can trust.
- A research and development agenda driven by public interest, not profit.

### 2c. Why now
- The DSA is in force and regulators are actively looking for alternatives to demonstrate.
- The NGI programme is expanding with dedicated funding for public digital infrastructure.
- Public disillusionment with commercial platforms is at an all-time high.
- The technical community is ready — what is missing is coordination and capital.

---

## Section 3 — Coalition Model (2 slides)

### 3a. Who we are
- Anchor partners: [list from Chapter 2]
- Three working groups: Technical, Policy, Funding.
- Annual open assembly for community participation.

### 3b. How decisions get made
- Steering Committee: one seat per anchor partner.
- Transparent budgeting, published annually.
- No single organisation holds veto power.

---

## Section 4 — Funding Ask (2 slides)

### 4a. Budget summary

| Line | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Secretariat (1.5 FTE) | €120k | €130k | €130k | €380k |
| Infrastructure maintenance fund | €200k | €250k | €300k | €750k |
| Protocol & tooling development | €150k | €150k | €100k | €400k |
| Policy & research | €80k | €100k | €80k | €260k |
| Communications & community | €50k | €60k | €60k | €170k |
| **Total** | **€600k** | **€690k** | **€670k** | **€1.96M** |

### 4b. Revenue model (years 2–3)
- Membership fees from institutional partners (municipalities, broadcasters): €5–20k/year per partner.
- Public grants (NGI, DFF, NWO): project-specific.
- Service contracts (SLA, compliance support) for public sector deployments.
- Goal: 40% self-sustaining by end of year 3.

---

## Section 5 — Impact (2 slides)

### 5a. Quantitative targets (3-year)

| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| Active Fediverse accounts | 25M (from 10M) |
| Institutional deployments supported | 20+ |
| Infrastructure uptime (coalition-supported nodes) | 99.5% |
| Policy submissions / consultations | 10+ |
| FOSS contributions funded | 50+ |

### 5b. Public value narrative
The Fediverse coalition is not building a product — it is maintaining a commons. The impact is measured not in users acquired but in:

- **Digital resilience:** Communities that cannot be deplatformed.
- **Civic infrastructure:** Public institutions with a social channel they control.
- **Democratic discourse:** Conversation spaces governed by communities, not algorithms.
- **European sovereignty:** Infrastructure that keeps European social data in European hands.
